rawPreview.text: Successful tool call result 0 with tool read_webpage: {"success": true, "data": {"content": "The Strait of Hormuz is a critical global oil chokepoint, connecting the Persian Gulf to the Arabian Sea and handling 20 million barrels per day (b/d) of oil in 2024—equivalent to ~20% of global petroleum liquids consumption. In Q1 2025, flows remained stable despite regional tensions, though Brent crude prices briefly rose to $74/b in mid-June 2025 due to heightened risk perceptions. Over one-quarter of global seaborne oil trade and ~20% of global oil and petroleum product consumption transited the strait in 2024; it also carried ~20% of global liquefied natural gas (LNG), primarily from Qatar. Saudi Arabia was the top exporter through the strait, accounting for 38% (5.5 million b/d) of crude and condensate flows in 2024, followed by the UAE and Iran. Between 2022–2024, Hormuz crude flows declined by 1.6 million b/d due to OPEC+ production cuts and increased regional refining, partially offset by a 0.5 million b/d rise in petroleum product exports. Alternative routes exist but have limited capacity: Saudi Arabia’s East-West pipeline (5–7 million b/d capacity) rerouted crude to the Red Sea amid Bab al-Mandeb disruptions; the UAE’s 1.8 million b/d Fujairah pipeline saw increased usage due to local refining upgrades, reducing spare capacity; Iran’s 300,000 b/d Goreh-Jask pipeline operated at <70,000 b/d in 2024 and was inactive after September. Approximately 84% of crude and 83% of LNG transiting Hormuz went to Asia—led by China, India, Japan, and South Korea, which together received 69% of Hormuz crude. The U.S. imported 0.5 million b/d from the Persian Gulf in 2024, its lowest level in nearly 40 years, due to rising domestic and Canadian supply. rawPreview.text: Successful tool call result 1 with tool read_webpage: {"success": true, "data": {"content": "The Gulf shipping crisis intensified as the Strait of Hormuz remained paralyzed for a fifth day, with over 200 tankers and cargo vessels stranded due to escalating U.S.-Iran hostilities. A U.S. submarine strike sank an Iranian warship off Sri Lanka, widening the conflict and triggering Iranian threats to fully close the vital waterway, through which 20% of global oil and LNG flows. At least eight vessels have been damaged since the conflict began on March 1, including the Maltese-flagged Safeen Prestige, which was hit by a projectile and abandoned, and the Libra Trader and Gold Oak, which sustained minor damage near Fujairah. Qatar halted all LNG liquefaction and declared force majeure, while Iraq cut oil production due to lack of storage and loading capacity. Saudi Aramco’s Ras Tanura terminal was also struck. U.S. President Donald Trump pledged political-risk insurance and naval escorts for Gulf shipping, but experts dismissed this as insufficient, citing the scale of required military assets. Commercial war risk insurance premiums surged fivefold. Oil prices rose 12% since the conflict began, with Goldman Sachs raising its Q2 Brent forecast to $76/barrel and WTI to $71/barrel, citing prolonged Hormuz disruptions and production cuts. Asian refiners, including India’s MRPL and Petronet LNG, issued force majeure notices as they struggled to secure replacement cargoes; Indonesia and Japan turned to U.S. oil, while India considered Russian imports. The region sources 60% of its oil from the Gulf, making it highly vulnerable. Despite the gridlock, one tanker, the Pola, transited Hormuz after disabling its AIS tracker. BIMCO warned that securing all Gulf vessels is logistically unfeasible.", "url": "https://today.reuters.com/business/energy/hormuz-shutdown-worsens-after-us-hits-iranian-warship-tankers-stranded-fifth-day-2026-03-04/"}}

rawPreview.text: Successful tool call result 0 with tool read_webpage: {"success": true, "data": {"content": "Aramco CEO Amin Nasser warned that prolonged disruption of oil exports through the Strait of Hormuz could delay global oil market recovery until 2027. With the strait—normally handling 20% of global oil supply—effectively closed due to Iran-related conflict, around 100 million barrels of oil are being lost weekly, and only 2–5 vessels are crossing daily versus the usual 70. Nasser emphasized that demand is not destroyed but rationed, and a robust rebound is expected once shipping resumes. Even if the strait reopened today, rebalancing would take months. To mitigate the crisis, Aramco has increased Red Sea exports via the East-West pipeline to Yanbu, now handling 60–70% of its crude volumes, and is exploring capacity expansion beyond its current 5 million bpd limit. Refined product exports via western terminals have reached nearly 900,000 bpd to capture higher margins. Key refineries like SAMREF and Ras Tanura are operational or recovering, while SATORP remains partially online. Aramco can ramp up to its 12 million bpd maximum sustainable crude capacity in under three weeks. The disruption has triggered surging energy prices, inflation fears, and economic uncertainty, with Asian markets turning to coal and European refiners gaining competitive advantage. rawPreview.text: Successful tool call result 1 with tool read_webpage: {"success": true, "data": {"content": "Transit through the Strait of Hormuz increased in late April 2026, peaking at 20 crossings on April 29, but remains uneven and enforcement-driven. Daily volumes fluctuated significantly: 19 crossings on April 25, dropped to 8 on April 26, rose to 13 on April 27–28, then jumped to 20 on April 29. Corridor use diversified, with 7 vessels using the Southern Corridor on April 29—its highest recent usage—indicating adaptive routing to mitigate risk. Gulf-wide vessel presence remained elevated at 902 vessels by April 29, while “dark activity” surged to 170 events, including AIS spoofing, unauthorized anchoring, and staging near Kharg Island and Chabahar (5–7 dark tankers observed weekly). Iranian crude exports to Asia declined, with no VLCCs transiting the Strait of Malacca eastbound since April 24. Deceptive shipping practices intensified, including falsified flags and AIS manipulation off Basrah. U.S. enforcement extended beyond the Gulf, intercepting the sanctioned LPG tanker LPG SEVAN in the Arabian Sea. GPS jamming near Khor Fakkan and IUU fishing in Oman’s EEZ added new operational risks. Despite resumed visible transit through controlled corridors, concealed activity persists around anchorages and alternative routes. The Strait is not closed, but movement is conditional, shaped by enforcement pressure, Iran’s constrained export flows, and evolving deceptive tactics. rawPreview.text: Successful tool call result 3 with tool read_webpage: {"success": true, "data": {"content": "Strait of Hormuz traffic remains drastically reduced (02 May: 4 transits vs. historical avg. 138/day), with heightened security pressure and sporadic GNSS/AIS interference. On 03 May 2026, a northbound bulk carrier was attacked by multiple small craft 11NM west of Sirik, Iran; all crew safe. Mine reports persist in TSS, and Iranian naval units engage in aggressive hailing and assertive maneuvers. No new verified kinetic incidents in last 72 hours, but 37 confirmed incidents since 1 March, including projectile/UAV strikes on tankers, bulkers, and rigs, primarily in Arabian Gulf and Gulf of Oman. In Gulf of Oman/Arabian Sea, elevated naval presence and UAV threats continue; blockade enforcement and electronic interference remain. Bab el-Mandeb/Gulf of Aden: no new maritime attacks; Houthi rhetoric persists without operational escalation. Somali Coast/Somali Basin threat level SEVERE: two vessels held by pirates (one off NE Somalia, one south of Eyl); a third tanker boarded ~10nm off Yemen is underway toward Somalia. A suspected PAG approached 500NM east of Somalia, closing to 400m before disengaging; three additional suspicious skiff approaches reported near Al Mukalla. Pirate activity enabled by hijacked dhows as motherships. Northern Red Sea/Suez Canal: stable, no attacks; sporadic EMI/GNSS interference. Eastern Mediterranean: no attacks, but periodic electronic interference near Cyprus/Levant; elevated naval presence; Gaza aid flotilla en route from Barcelona. Port congestion unchanged in Arabian Gulf anchorages; vessels advised to reposition to reduce predictability. Overall maritime risk level: CRITICAL. GNSS interference remains localized across Strait of Hormuz, Gulf of Oman, Arabian Gulf, central Red Sea, and Suez approaches. AIS reliability improved since mid-March. JMIC advises strict BMP-MS compliance, vigilant reporting to UKMTO, and avoidance of naval units. No infrastructure attacks reported. Traffic data from S&P Maritime and SeaVision.", "url": "https://www.ukmto.org/-/media/ukmto/products/update-040-jmic-advisory-note-03-may.pdf?rev=553ce09bac634216a8202747afcbdc28"}}

rawPreview.text: Successful tool call result 0 with tool read_webpage: {"success": true, "data": {"content": "Despite a U.S. pledge to restore freedom of navigation, shipping through the Strait of Hormuz remains at a standstill as of May 4, 2026. MarineTraffic data showed only one sanctioned LPG tanker and a handful of cargo vessels transited into the Gulf of Oman, with no commercial tankers queued for passage. Major shipping firms like Hapag-Lloyd reported transit as “impossible” due to unclear security protocols. The U.S. Central Command (CENTCOM) announced defensive operations to support navigation but provided no operational guidance to the industry. The Baltic and International Maritime Council (BIMCO) warned that without Iran’s consent, the threat to vessels cannot be suppressed. Iran has demanded all commercial ships coordinate passage with its military and issued a new map asserting control over the strait, while warning the U.S. Navy to stay out. The U.S.-led naval blockade on Iranian ports, imposed April 13, has drastically reduced Iran’s oil exports, forcing crude onto floating storage. The International Maritime Organization estimates hundreds of vessels and up to 20,000 seafarers are stranded. The maritime threat level remains “critical,” with mariners advised to route south of the traffic separation scheme via Omani waters. Webpage content:
The UAE’s eastern ports of Fujairah and Khor Fakkan have become critical lifelines for Gulf trade amid the closure of the Strait of Hormuz due to the Iran conflict. Fujairah’s crude exports surged 38% to 1.62 million barrels per day, nearing its pipeline capacity of 1.5–1.8 million bpd, while container traffic has newly emerged. Khor Fakkan experienced a 25-fold increase in container volumes—rising from 2,000 to 50,000 weekly containers—with daily vessel calls nearly quadrupling. Terminal operator Gulftainer hired 900 workers in two weeks and is building a $100 million inland logistics hub in Al Dhaid, linked by road and rail. Truck movements at Khor Fakkan jumped from 100 to 7,000 per day. Despite Iranian drone strikes on Fujairah’s oil zone in March and again on May 4, injuring three workers and damaging infrastructure, operations continue, though congestion is severe. Iran’s Revolutionary Guards published a map claiming control over the eastern UAE coast, signaling threats to the ports. The UAE, relying on these ports to bypass Hormuz, has stressed its right to defend trade routes. Other Gulf states like Saudi Arabia use alternative routes (e.g., Yanbu via the East-West Pipeline), but Iran also struck those in spring. Qatar, Kuwait, and Bahrain remain heavily dependent on Khor Fakkan or costly overland routes through Saudi Arabia and Oman. The region’s trade architecture remains fragile, exposed to direct military threats, with no clear timeline for Hormuz reopening. Regional cooperation on alternative corridors is emerging as a contingency, but the UAE’s eastern ports now serve not just national but regional economic survival."}}

Webpage content:
Week 11 (9–15 Mar 2026) JMIC Dashboard highlights critical maritime security conditions in the Middle East. Threat levels remain MODERATE in the Red Sea (RS), Bab el-Mandeb (BAM), and Gulf of Aden (GoA), but CRITICAL in the Arabian Gulf, Gulf of Oman, and Strait of Hormuz (SoH). SoH remains open for transit. No piracy incidents reported; piracy threat in GoA/Somali Basin/Indian Ocean is LOW. Houthi-related incidents were unreported this week, though Houthi threats persist in the Red Sea. Electromagnetic Interference (EMI) is CRITICAL in central Red Sea, Bandar-e-Pars, Fujairah, Khor Fakkan, Dubai, Abu Dhabi, and Bandar Abbas; severe to critical levels persist in Strait of Hormuz and southern Arabian Gulf, while northern areas show reduced interference. Vessels are urged to report EMI to UKMTO and verify navigation via non-GNSS means. Six serious attacks and one near-miss occurred (09–15 Mar); 148 total investigated incidents since 2025 (none new this week). Last serious attack: 12 Mar 2026 (bulk carrier, tanker, container vessel). Transits sharply declined: SoH down 56.4%, BAM down 19.5%, Suez down 6.8%, CoGH down 1.4% compared to prior week—reflecting ongoing regional hostilities since Feb 2026. No naval mining confirmed in SoH. No active PAGs noted, possibly due to weather. UKMTO/JMIC continue operations; vessels must report suspicious activity. Mitigation tools: BMP-Marketing Security, JMIC Bridge Emergency Cards, ASPIDES registration. Definitions clarify incident types: “Attack (Serious)” = significant damage impairing vessel function; “Attempted Attack” = targeted but no damage; “Suspicious Activity” = unconfirmed but concerning behavior. Data excludes local vessels; only international cargo ships counted."}}

Webpage content:
Operational uncertainty persists in the Strait of Hormuz despite a U.S.-Iran ceasefire announced on April 8, 2026. On April 7, 11 vessels transited the strait—four inbound (all sanctioned Iranian-trading tankers) and seven outbound (three tankers, four bulk carriers), all using the Northern Corridor through Iranian-controlled waters, bypassing the international channel. Transit volumes and risk profiles remained unchanged post-ceasefire. Iran maintains de facto control, requiring all transits to coordinate with the IRGC, with unconfirmed “technical limitations” governing passage. Iran demands tolls—$1 per barrel of oil for inbound shipments, paid in cryptocurrency—and insists on vessel inspections and prior approval, warning that unauthorized ships will be targeted. Radio broadcasts confirm enforcement. The April 7 attack on the Maersk-chartered Qingdao Star by an IRGC Qadir cruise missile—despite no injuries—demonstrates ongoing strikes during ceasefire negotiations. The vessel, previously chartered by Israeli ZIM, was misidentified as Israeli, highlighting risks for vessels with past Israeli links. No blue-chip operators or major oil majors are transiting; only risk-tolerant firms are active. ~3,200 vessels (~800 tankers/cargos) and 20,000 seafarers remain stranded west of the strait. Insurance war-risk exclusions remain a major barrier. A southern corridor via Oman has seen no traffic since mid-March. Oman refuses to pay tolls, signaling diplomatic friction. Iran’s conditions—U.S. military withdrawal, recognition of enrichment rights, full sanctions lift—remain unmet. The critical window for normalization is April 8–14: daily transit increases without incidents may prompt blue-chip reassessment, but full recovery requires weeks to months. The strait remains in a “supervised pause,” not reopened. Full normalization is not imminent."}}

Webpage content:
The United States and Iran remain deeply divided in negotiations to end their ongoing war and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, a critical global oil transit route. President Donald Trump has rejected Iran’s latest peace proposal, which demanded the immediate lifting of Washington’s naval blockade and comprehensive sanctions relief, while allowing Iran to retain significant control over maritime traffic through the strait. In response, Trump is considering reviving a plan to deploy U.S. naval escorts to secure shipping lanes independently, bypassing Iranian oversight. Iran insists that any deal must include full sanctions relief and recognition of its sovereignty over Hormuz, while the U.S. refuses to concede operational control or lift sanctions without verifiable de-escalation and guarantees on nuclear activities. The deadlock has prolonged the conflict, exacerbating global energy markets: oil buffers are being drained at an unprecedented rate, jet fuel shortages are triggering airline cancellations and fare hikes, and regional instability continues to escalate. Diplomatic efforts are stalled, with neither side willing to compromise on core demands—sanctions relief versus security control—raising fears of prolonged conflict and further disruption to global trade."}}

User query:
Summarize this Reuters Open Interest commentary focusing on whether the Strait of Hormuz is still effectively blocked/disrupted as of May 12, 2026, what military or diplomatic dynamics matter next, and whether reopening looks imminent within the coming week.

Webpage content:
The Strait of Hormuz has become the epicenter of a escalating Iran conflict, following joint Israeli-U.S. airstrikes on February 28 and a subsequent U.S. naval blockade, which nearly halted all tanker traffic through the chokepoint. The strait, through which 13% of global oil and 20% of LNG flows pass daily, has seen a dramatic drop from ~140 vessels to just a handful, triggering global energy market volatility. Recent limited transits—three Iraqi VLCCs carrying 2 million barrels each and two Qatari LNG cargoes to Pakistan—have occurred with transponders switched off, suggesting tacit Iranian approval or informal tolls. These movements, while offering temporary relief to import-dependent Asian economies, signal a dangerous new norm: Iran now controls who can transit, not merely whether the strait is open. This selective access undermines global energy security and challenges U.S. war aims, as President Trump insists on restoring pre-war freedom of navigation. Iran, having suffered an estimated $3 billion in lost revenue, has little incentive to relinquish its leverage, viewing Hormuz as its most potent geopolitical weapon. The U.S. and Gulf states, particularly Saudi Arabia and the UAE, oppose any permanent arrangement granting Tehran veto power over energy flows. A ceasefire may stabilize the frontlines but is unlikely to restore full, unconditional access. Instead, a fragile, Iran-managed stasis risks institutionalizing disruption, making renewed conflict—between Iran and the U.S., or Iran and Gulf allies—increasingly probable. The long-term outcome hinges on who controls the strait, a question that will define the future of Gulf geopolitics and global energy stability."}}

Webpage content:
Ships continue to wait in the Strait of Hormuz amid heightened tensions between the U.S. and Iran, though both sides appear to be moving toward a ceasefire. President Trump described the potential Iran ceasefire as being on “life support,” while simultaneously proposing a pause on the federal gasoline tax—a move he cannot enact unilaterally. The Strait remains closed, disrupting global oil shipments and contributing to energy market volatility. Meanwhile, Asia is bracing for a second wave of energy shocks linked to the Iran conflict, and California, Nevada, and Arizona have implemented a temporary plan to conserve water from the Colorado River. In other U.S. news, Trump nominated Cameron Hamilton, previously fired for defending FEMA, to lead the agency; the Supreme Court halted Alabama’s congressional map requiring two majority-Black districts; and China’s passenger car exports surged nearly 85% in April as domestic sales declined. The FDA approved its first fruit-flavored e-cigarettes for adults, marking a policy shift under Trump. On the health front, experts raised concerns over the CDC’s response to a hantavirus outbreak on a cruise ship, and social media health claims are being scrutinized. Additional global developments include a record-breaking El Niño forecast, a viral trend in Argentina where youth identify as animals, and Fatou, the world’s oldest captive gorilla, celebrating her 69th birthday in Berlin."}}

actions.title: Record forecast
actions.summary: 截至5月12日，多家独立来源一致显示霍尔木兹已处于持续多日的closure-class级别停摆：交通量接近归零、商业油轮基本停止、且重开谈判仍陷僵局。距离5月19日仅一周，现有轨迹更像延续封闭态势而非快速回到仅‘严重扰动’。
actions.argsSummary: prediction=D · confidence=medium

forecastPayload.rationale: 截至5月12日，多家独立来源一致显示霍尔木兹已处于持续多日的closure-class级别停摆：交通量接近归零、商业油轮基本停止、且重开谈判仍陷僵局。距离5月19日仅一周，现有轨迹更像延续封闭态势而非快速回到仅‘严重扰动’。
keyEvidenceItems: 路透5月4日报道称尽管美国承诺恢复航行自由，但海峡航运“仍处于停滞”，没有商业油轮排队通行。
UKMTO/JMIC 5月5日与5月7日通报将霍尔木兹及周边威胁评为CRITICAL，记录到通行量从历史约138艘/日暴跌至4艘甚至0艘，并伴随袭击、投射物命中、GNSS/AIS干扰等。
Safety4Sea/Windward与AP均描述海峡为‘supervised pause’/‘remains closed’，只有高风险承受者或受伊朗条件约束的船舶极少量通过，数千船员和大量船舶仍被困。
路透5月12日评论与彭博5月11日报道都显示重开谈判仍然僵持，未来一周内恢复到可自由商业通行的概率不高。
counterEvidenceItems: 路透5月12日评论提到已有少数VLCC和卡塔尔LNG货船在伊朗默许下进行有限、选择性通行，说明并非绝对零流量。
美伊仍在谈判，AP与其他报道都提到停火/和谈并未完全破裂；若未来一周突然达成护航或放行安排，状态可能从D回落到C。
openConcerns: 若美伊在5月12日至19日之间突然达成临时护航、停火执行或放行协议，状态可能转为C而非D。
题目未指定唯一裁决源；若最终判定更强调‘存在少量受控通行’而不是‘商业自由通行是否停摆’，C与D的边界可能产生解释争议。
temporalNotes: 核心证据覆盖到2026-05-12，距离目标日2026-05-19约一周，属于短期外推。
UKMTO/JMIC、路透、AP均可直接读取；部分彭博页面存在403/验证码，但已被其他来源交叉印证。

sourceObservations.title: Galaxy final prediction
sourceObservations.summary: The question asks which categorical transit-risk scenario will best describe the Strait of Hormuz on 2026-05-19, with the practical challenge being to infer the likely case state one week ahead from current trajectory rather than find a direct answer. The run established that by 2026-05-12 the strait was still operating at a closure-class level: official/advisory sources showed near-zero traffic and critical threat conditions, while Reuters, AP, Bloomberg, and Windward all described a shipping environment where ordinary commercial transit was largely frozen, only limited selective passage occurred, and negotiations to reopen remained unresolved.
